Heart Hackathon 2026

Engineering the Future of Cardiac Care

Pulse Project at UCI is a student-led team of undergraduates developing a Total Artificial Heart to provide a meaningful bridge to transplant for patients in critical need.

The TAH
See full design →
Our TAH

A Dual-Chamber Axial Flow Heart

One integrated device, two circulations — engineered for physiological realism and blood safety.

How It Works

Blood enters each chamber — left from the vena cavae, right from pulmonary return — and passes through a stator that converts turbulent inflow into laminar flow. A rotor then accelerates it into the pulmonary and systemic circulations, with the left chamber tuned for higher pressure output.

🫀
CAD render — dual-chamber assembly
(coming soon)
🌀
Blade & stator geometry
(SolidWorks model)

Why Axial Flow?

One rotating axle, no valves, small footprint. Pressure is shaped entirely by blade geometry — simpler to fabricate, assemble, and keep blood-safe.

5.2±1.1 L/min
Cardiac Output
138±17 mmHg
Systemic Pressure
37±10 mmHg
Pulmonary Pressure
>80% / ≥3
Efficiency / Safety Factor
📡
Electrical architecture diagram

Control System

A brushless DC motor with a motor-derived flow sensor and MEMS pressure sensor feed a closed-loop ESP32 controller, using PID and model-predictive control to hold stable output as conditions change.

How We Compare

Existing Total Artificial Hearts

SynCardia

  • 12 L/min flow
  • External pneumatic driver
  • High infection risk

BiVACOR

  • Fully implantable
  • High energy demand
  • Complex maglev control

AbioCor

  • Lightweight, biocompatible
  • TETS electromagnetic risk
  • Large size

Carmat

  • No external actuator
  • Durability concerns
  • High thrombotic risk
Strategic Outlook

SWOT

Strengths

  • Valve-less design lowers thrombosis risk
  • Motor-derived flow sensing
  • Compact footprint

Weaknesses

  • Simulating heartbeat at continuous flow
  • High-pressure generation
  • Battery-free power supply

Opportunities

  • Scalable manufacturing
  • Growing bridge-to-transplant demand
  • Better biomaterials

Threats

  • Dense patent landscape
  • Class III PMA regulatory path
  • Established competitors
Roadmap

Path to Grand Finals

Mar – Apr 2026

Team Formation & Research

Defined design requirements, explored pump architectures

May 2026

System Architecture

Mechanical & electrical integration

June 2026

Design & Simulation

Full CAD models, V-Patient software testing

Jul – Aug 2026

Fabrication

Materials & manufacturing

Aug – Sep 2026

Testing & Iteration

Prototype refinement

Oct 2026

Grand Finals

Final prototype presentation
